terms.definition: Structured intervention using graded sensory input to improve a child's processing and adaptive responses.
terms.exampleSentence: The child engaged well with sensory integration therapy in the ball pit and swing.
terms.contextNotes: Evidence base debated; document goals clearly.

terms.definition: Therapy aimed at restoring or compensating for cognitive deficits following brain injury or stroke.
terms.exampleSentence: Cognitive rehabilitation focused on attention and executive function tasks.
terms.contextNotes: Use validated outcome measures such as MoCA.

terms.definition: Fabrication and fitting of a custom orthosis to support, immobilise or position a joint or limb.
terms.exampleSentence: Splinting was provided to maintain wrist extension and prevent contracture.
terms.contextNotes: Document wear schedule and skin checks.

terms.definition: Externally applied devices used to modify the structural or functional characteristics of the musculoskeletal system.
terms.exampleSentence: She was referred to orthotics for assessment of an ankle-foot orthosis.
terms.contextNotes: Distinguish from prosthetics (replace limb).

terms.definition: Strategies that reduce fatigue by pacing tasks, prioritising activities and using efficient movement.
terms.exampleSentence: Energy conservation techniques were taught to manage post-stroke fatigue.
terms.contextNotes: Often called the four Ps: plan, prioritise, pace, posture.

terms.definition: Modifying tasks and the environment to reduce effort and complete activities more efficiently.
terms.exampleSentence: Work simplification advice was provided for kitchen tasks at home.
terms.contextNotes: Pair with energy conservation in fatigue plans.

terms.definition: Principles that reduce stress on inflamed or unstable joints through positioning, larger joints and assistive devices.
terms.exampleSentence: Joint protection education was delivered as part of the rheumatology pathway.
terms.contextNotes: Especially used in inflammatory arthritis care.

terms.definition: Changes to the home, workplace or community that enable participation despite functional limitations.
terms.exampleSentence: Environmental modification included widening doorways and installing a wet room.
terms.contextNotes: Major adaptations may need DFG funding in the UK.

terms.definition: Multifactorial intervention reducing the risk of falls through strength, balance, vision and home safety review.
terms.exampleSentence: She was referred to the community falls prevention service after two unwitnessed falls.
terms.contextNotes: NICE recommends multifactorial assessment for ≥65s.

terms.definition: Teaching safe techniques to move between surfaces such as bed to chair, chair to toilet or car transfers.
terms.exampleSentence: Transfer training using a banana board enabled independent toilet transfers.
terms.contextNotes: Document level of assistance and equipment used.

terms.definition: Specialist assessment matching a wheelchair and seating system to the user's posture, function and environment.
terms.exampleSentence: Wheelchair prescription included a tilt-in-space frame and pressure-relieving cushion.
terms.contextNotes: Refer to wheelchair services for complex needs.

terms.definition: Augmentative and alternative communication: tools and strategies that supplement or replace speech.
terms.exampleSentence: An AAC assessment was arranged for the patient with motor neurone disease.
terms.contextNotes: Joint working with speech and language therapy.

terms.definition: An individualised schedule of sensory activities designed to maintain optimal arousal and attention.
terms.exampleSentence: A sensory diet of heavy work activities was introduced before classroom tasks.
terms.contextNotes: Often co-designed with parents and teachers.

terms.definition: Therapeutic strategies including grading, graded exposure and relaxation to reduce anxiety in daily activities.
terms.exampleSentence: Anxiety management techniques were practised before community outings.
terms.contextNotes: OT often co-delivers with mental health team.

terms.definition: Systematic, progressive practice of feared activities to reduce avoidance and rebuild confidence.
terms.exampleSentence: Graded exposure to public transport was started after the panic disorder diagnosis.
terms.contextNotes: Build hierarchy collaboratively with the patient.

terms.definition: Structured therapeutic session delivered to several patients sharing similar goals or conditions.
terms.exampleSentence: She attended weekly upper-limb group therapy on the stroke unit.
terms.contextNotes: Document individual goals within group context.

terms.definition: Process supporting people with health conditions to remain in, return to or obtain suitable employment.
terms.exampleSentence: Vocational rehabilitation included a graded return-to-work plan agreed with the employer.
terms.contextNotes: Liaison with occupational health is common.

terms.definition: Targeted activities that address visual-perceptual or body-scheme deficits, often after stroke or brain injury.
terms.exampleSentence: Perceptual retraining addressed left unilateral neglect during dressing.
terms.contextNotes: Combine top-down and bottom-up approaches.

terms.definition: Use of CBT-informed strategies in OT to modify unhelpful thoughts and behaviours affecting occupation.
terms.exampleSentence: A cognitive behavioural approach helped her challenge avoidance of meal preparation.
terms.contextNotes: Stay within scope; refer for formal CBT if needed.

terms.definition: Structured intervention increasing engagement in meaningful activities to improve mood and function.
terms.exampleSentence: Behavioural activation focused on re-engaging with gardening and walking the dog.
terms.contextNotes: Especially useful in low-mood presentations.

terms.definition: Technique that works around an impairment, using preserved abilities or external aids to complete tasks.
terms.exampleSentence: Compensatory strategies such as a memory diary supported safe medication management.
terms.contextNotes: Contrast with restorative (remediative) approach.

terms.definition: Intervention aiming to restore underlying impairments rather than compensate for them.
terms.exampleSentence: A remedial approach to grip strength was used alongside compensatory equipment.
terms.contextNotes: Often combined with compensation in mixed plans.

terms.definition: Therapeutic task adjusted in demand to provide just-right challenge and progress function.
terms.exampleSentence: Graded activity in the kitchen progressed from making tea to preparing a hot meal.
terms.contextNotes: Core OT skill across all settings.

terms.definition: Breaking down an occupation into its components to understand demands and identify intervention points.
terms.exampleSentence: Activity analysis of dressing identified balance and reach as key barriers.
terms.contextNotes: Foundational to OT clinical reasoning.

terms.definition: Intensive upper-limb therapy restraining the unaffected arm to drive use of the affected limb after stroke.
terms.exampleSentence: Constraint-induced movement therapy was trialled to improve right hand function post-stroke.
terms.contextNotes: Requires minimum active wrist/finger extension.
